blurb: Heat-loving celosia throws up velvety flame-like plumes or brain-folded crested combs in electric reds, oranges, and golds that shrug off the hottest Front Range summer.
water: Low to moderate; drought- and heat-tolerant once established
spread: Upright clump; does not run, may self-sow lightly in warm sites
seasons: Plumed or crested flowerheads from early summer to first frost, holding color for weeks; frost-tender foliage; killed by the first hard freeze
wildlife: Flowers draw bees, butterflies, and other pollinators; seedheads feed small birds
deer: Not deer-resistant — deer will browse it
toxic: Non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses; leaves and young shoots are eaten cooked
habitat: Warm disturbed ground, fields, and roadsides in the tropics (weedy in parts of its range)

care.hardiness: Reliably perennial only in USDA zones 10–11, so on the Front Range it is grown as a warm-season annual and is killed by the first hard frost.[1]
care.planting: A frost-tender heat-lover: do not plant out or sow until well after Boulder's average last frost (about May 10–15) and the soil and nights have truly warmed — cold soil stalls and yellows young celosia.[3] Start seed indoors 4–6 weeks before that and harden off, or direct-sow once warm. It thrives in Front Range heat once established. In containers use a sunny, well-drained pot; potted plants dry faster in our heat and wind, so water more often. There is no overwintering outdoors — it dies at frost.[1][3]
care.sun: Full sun (6+ hours) for the strongest stems and most intense plume/comb color; it needs the heat.[1]
care.soil: Average to lean, well-drained soil; tolerates dry, porous ground and a wide pH (roughly 6.0–8.0), so Boulder's alkaline clay is fine if drainage is improved. Avoid soggy soil, which rots the roots.[1]
care.water: Low to moderate — celosia is drought- and heat-tolerant once established. Keep seedlings evenly moist, then water deeply but allow the surface to dry between soakings. It actively prefers heat and is not fussy about humidity.[1]
care.spacing: Space plants 9–18 in. apart depending on the cultivar (dwarf bedding types closer, tall cut-flower types wider); each forms an upright clump 9 in.–3 ft tall.[1]
care.sow: Start seed indoors about 4–6 weeks before the last frost, or direct-sow after frost once soil is warm (mid-to-late May in Boulder). Press the fine seed onto the surface and barely cover; warmth speeds germination.[1][3]
care.depth: Sow shallow — the seed is small and benefits from light, so cover with no more than about 1/8–1/4 in. of fine soil and keep it warm and moist.[1]
care.bloom: Early summer through the first frost — the plumed and crested heads hold their color for many weeks (the heads stay attractive for up to ~8 weeks).[1][4]
care.feeding: Light feeding only; rich, high-nitrogen soil produces leafy growth at the expense of strong, well-colored flower heads. Lean soil gives better plumes.[1]
care.maintenance: Low. Deadhead spent heads to encourage more bloom on plumed types; tall cut-flower selections may need staking in windy spots. Pull and compost after frost.[1]
care.selfsow: May self-sow lightly in warm, open ground, but Front Range frost limits volunteers; seedlings are easy to thin. (The species is a weed in some tropical regions, but is not a Colorado-listed weed and is frost-killed here.)[1][4]
care.harvest: For dried arrangements, cut the heads when fully colored but before seed sets, strip the leaves, and hang upside down in a dark, airy spot to dry — celosia holds its color well dried. For greens, pick young leaves and shoots through summer.[1][2]
care.propagation: Usually grown from seed (started indoors or direct-sown); stem cuttings can also root in warm conditions. Collect ripe seed from dried heads in fall and sow the next spring after frost.[1]

edible.summary: The young leaves and shoots of Celosia argentea are eaten cooked as a leafy vegetable — 'Lagos spinach' (soko) across West Africa — but garden cultivars are bred for show, not the table, and the greens are eaten cooked, not raw.[2][4]
edible.parts: The young leaves and tender shoots are the food part: an important, nutritious cooked vegetable used in soups and stews in West Africa and Southeast Asia, mild and spinach-like with no bitterness when cooked. An edible oil can be pressed from the seed.[2][4]
edible.preparation: Cook the leaves and shoots — they soften readily and should not be overcooked, holding a pleasant green color; they are added to soups, stews, and boiled-green dishes, not eaten as a raw salad.[2]
edible.caution: Eat only the young leaves/shoots and only cooked; ornamental bedding and cut-flower celosias are selected for flower form and color, not for food quality, so treat homegrown ornamental plants as a leafy green of last resort rather than a deliberate vegetable. Be certain of your identification. The plant is non-toxic to people and to dogs, cats, and horses, and PFAF lists no known culinary hazard, but it carries a medicinal caution against use by people with glaucoma (it can dilate the pupils) — that is a herbal-medicine concern, not an issue for cooked greens.[1][2]
